meta.scene_summary: We are building the future with new tools just to watch the old security holes burn it down.

cooking.headline: OpenSSH 10.4 is out, because your server access deserves a fresh layer of anxiety
cooking.summary: Another OpenSSH update is here to ensure that your remote access stays secure while you frantically check if your current config will break. It is the digital equivalent of changing the locks on your house every few months just to keep the neighborhood ghosts guessing.
cooking.plain_english: OpenSSH has released version 10.4, which includes updates and security improvements for the software used to manage secure remote connections to servers.

cooking.headline: The EU is trying to break end-to-end encryption, because why should privacy be easy?
cooking.summary: Lawmakers are pushing to scan encrypted chats, effectively turning every private message into a group project with a government auditor. It is the digital equivalent of demanding a spare key to every house in the city just in case someone decides to bake a cake illegally.
cooking.plain_english: The European Parliament has advanced a proposal that would require messaging services to scan private user communications for illegal content.

cooking.headline: Odin 1.0 just dropped for developers who think C is too easy
cooking.summary: Odin has officially hit 1.0, proving that the tech world will never stop building a better version of the language we already spent a decade learning. It's the perfect choice if you want to write high-performance code while explaining to your friends why you refuse to use anything with a garbage collector.
cooking.plain_english: The programming language Odin has reached its first stable release version.

cooking.headline: Kani is here to remind you that your Rust code is only 'safe' until you actually run it
cooking.summary: Rust promises memory safety, but Kani exists because sometimes your logic is still a chaotic disaster waiting to happen. It is essentially a debugger that judges your life choices before the compiler even lets you deploy.
cooking.plain_english: Kani is a verification tool that checks Rust programs for potential logical errors and bugs before the code is executed.

cooked.headline: Januscape lets guests break into the host, proving virtualization is just a suggestion
cooked.summary: Virtualization is supposed to be the ultimate digital 'do not disturb' sign, but this exploit lets guests walk right through the front door. It turns out your secure isolation layer is currently about as effective as a screen door on a submarine.
cooked.plain_english: A new security vulnerability allows a virtual machine to escape its isolated environment and take control of the physical computer hosting it.

cooked.headline: Meta just hit a $1.4 trillion legal wall, and the lawyers are definitely not billing by the hour.
cooked.summary: Zuckerberg is facing a legal nightmare so expensive it makes a mid-life crisis look like a rounding error. At this point, the fine is large enough that it might be easier for Meta to just buy a small country and move the headquarters there.
cooked.plain_english: Meta is facing a potential legal penalty of up to 1.4 trillion dollars due to a significant new lawsuit.

cooked.headline: Supreme Court might turn the panopticon off
cooked.summary: Flock built a massive surveillance empire by selling plate-reading cameras to everyone with a budget. Now the Supreme Court is looking at whether your private movements are a matter of public record, which is a massive plot twist for their business model.
cooked.plain_english: The United States Supreme Court is reviewing the legal status of private companies that collect and share license plate data with law enforcement agencies.

cooked.headline: Shipping 37k lines of AI code a day is just fancy copy-pasting
cooked.summary: The YC CEO claimed his AI agents were shipping 37,000 lines of code daily, but it turns out they were mostly just hallucinating repetitive boilerplate. It turns out that 'agentic coding' is just the digital equivalent of a student copy-pasting the same paragraph fifty times to hit a word count.
cooked.plain_english: An analysis of code produced by automated AI agents revealed that much of the output was redundant or low-quality, contradicting claims about the volume of meaningful work completed.

cooked.headline: Tech CEOs finally realized AI might not actually replace their entire payroll
cooked.summary: After spending two years promising that AI would handle all the work, tech leaders are now backpedaling to claim it's actually a 'productivity tool.' It turns out that firing everyone is a lot easier to pitch in a deck than it is to manage when the server starts hallucinating your quarterly roadmap. More details in the original article.
cooked.plain_english: Technology company executives have shifted their public stance to suggest that artificial intelligence will assist human employees rather than replace them entirely.

cooked.headline: Microsoft's device ID is basically a permanent 'kick me' sign for your PC
cooked.summary: It turns out Windows has been keeping a digital paper trail so detailed that law enforcement can track you down just by looking at your hardware ID. It is the tech equivalent of leaving your GPS history open on a public monitor while trying to commit a crime.
cooked.plain_english: A legal case revealed that Microsoft can identify and track specific Windows users through a unique device hardware identifier.
